notes: searches: ~10 (8 shared, 2 stragglers: Scala Sancta modern specialist scholarship for GC-07-036; Melanchthon 'Histori Thome Muntzers' genealogy for GC-10-054). 2 WebFetch (Lutheran Quarterly PDF had no extractable text layer; Schaff HCC VII via CCEL). Local scholarship greps (no budget cost): D'Aubigne 1849 OCR file confirmed as the direct source layer for all four episodes -- Worms wording incl. 'Here I stand' at lines 40834-40851; Scala Sancta thunder-voice dramatization at lines 12709-12750; Muntzer 'dissatisfied with the inferior part he was playing' at lines 62215-62233; Luther's exculpatory 'those who have rightly understood my doctrine do not revolt' at lines 62073-62078. All four claims were defense-downgraded from anachronistic in Phase 1; per section 7.4 each defense argument is engaged explicitly below.
evidence_review: The dossier covers four D'Aubigne-mediated episodes of the Luther narrative. (1) Worms, 18 April 1521. Luther's substantive final answer -- popes and councils have erred, conscience captive to the word of God, 'I cannot and will not recant... God help me. Amen' -- is universally accepted; it survives in the official transcripts edited in Deutsche Reichstagsakten, Juengere Reihe II. The closing sentence 'Here I stand, I can do no other' is absent from the notarial on-the-spot records but present in the earliest Wittenberg printed edition of the speech (1521, weeks after the event), whence Melanchthon and the whole later tradition. The state of the question, per Samuel L. Young's peer-reviewed study 'How Luther Became the Mythical Here I Stand Hero' (Lutheran Quarterly 36, 2022) and the 500th-anniversary literature (Concordia Theology 2021; CPH; luther2017.de): for roughly half a century most scholars have held the words were 'probably not spoken', while conceding they may be genuine; Roland Bainton's standard biography defends possible authenticity ('the listeners at that moment may have been too moved to write'). Two contemporaneous witness classes conflict, and no scholar claims certainty: a genuine, unresolved dispute. (2) Scala Sancta. Luther verifiably climbed 'Pilate's staircase' on his Rome journey (redated 1511/12 by Hans Schneider, followed by Kaufmann, Moeller, Leppin, Koepf) seeking the indulgence, partly for his grandfather in purgatory; his own 1545 sermon recalls only doubt at the top ('Who knows whether it is true?'). The story that 'the just shall live by faith' came to him on the stairs rests on a single late, secondhand family source: his son Paul Luther's memorandum (preserved at Rudolstadt) of what his father narrated at table in 1544. Schaff (History of the Christian Church VII) reports and credits this attestation; modern specialist scholarship (Schneider; the De Gruyter volume 'Martin Luther in Rom', 2017; cf. Brecht vol. 1) doubts a reformatory awakening on the stairs and reads Paul's account as a harmonizing attempt to connect the later justification insight (dated by Luther's own 1545 preface to the Wittenberg years, c. 1518-19) with Rome. The thunder-voice, the springing up, and the flight 'in shame and horror' exist in no 16th-century source at all; they are D'Aubigne's dramatization, which GC compresses. (3) Peasants' War responsibility. What changed since the 19th century: Guenther Franz's older school read the revolt as a political struggle rooted in pre-Reformation traditions (Bundschuh, 'altes Recht'); Peter Blickle's The Revolution of 1525 established the now-standard view that the 1525 program was framed by the evangelical message ('godly law'; the Twelve Articles, co-drafted by the evangelical preacher Christoph Schappeler with Sebastian Lotzer, ground every demand in Scripture; peasants appealed to Luther's 'freedom of a Christian'), a line carried into Lyndal Roper's Summer of Fire and Blood (2025) and the OUP monograph Luther and the Peasants (2025). At the same time the whole field agrees Luther neither intended nor sanctioned revolt: the Admonition to Peace urged peace and blamed the lords too; Against the Robbing and Murdering Hordes condemned the rising; his two-kingdoms reading of Christian freedom excluded political insurrection ('a great and tragic misunderstanding' in the exculpatory literature: Kolb; McGath/OLL; the Saeculum article the Phase-1 defender cited). So the 19th-century flat exoneration ('no connection whatever', D'Aubigne) survives only in the intentional/doctrinal-warrant sense, not in the causal-contribution sense. (4) Muntzer's motives. The hostile portrait -- proud, ambitious, unwilling to be second -- descends from Luther's polemics and above all Melanchthon's Histori Thome Muntzers (1525), which scholarship (Abraham Friesen, Church History) treats as a humanist oratorical exemplar designed to show revolution as the natural fruit of Schwaermerei, not as history; D'Aubigne transmits exactly this frame ('dissatisfied with the inferior part he was playing'). Modern specialist biography (Tom Scott 1989; Hans-Juergen Goertz 1989/2015; Braeuer-Vogler 2016; Andrew Drummond 2024) grounds Muntzer's course in documented apocalyptic-mystical theology: his own Prague Manifesto (1521), Sermon to the Princes (1524), Vindication and Refutation (1524), and published correspondence (Matheson, Collected Works, 1988). The rivalry itself is undeniable and documented -- Muntzer refused subordination to Wittenberg and attacked Luther as 'Doctor Liar' and 'the soft-living flesh of Wittenberg' -- but the reading of it as careerist ambition for 'position and influence' has no documentary basis in Muntzer's corpus and is rejected as polemical caricature by the specialists.
narrative_ru: Досье объединяет четыре эпизода лютеровской истории, которые «Великая борьба» пересказывает по Д'Обинье. Знаменитая фраза «На сём стою» есть в первом печатном издании речи 1521 года, но отсутствует в протоколах рейхстага, и учёные до сих пор расходятся, произнёс ли её Лютер; суть его ответа в Вормсе подлинна. Сцена на Святой лестнице с «голосом как гром» и характеристика Мюнцера как честолюбца в записанном виде не выдержали проверки: первая опирается на позднее семейное предание, драматизированное Д'Обинье, вторая противоречит документированным апокалиптико-богословским мотивам самого Мюнцера. Абсолютное отрицание «малейшего основания» у обвинения Лютера в Крестьянской войне также расходится с современной историографией, хотя сам Лютер восстание осуждал и к нему не призывал. В ослабленных формулировках три из четырёх утверждений хорошо подтверждены, а картину изменили бы новые документы XVI века: независимое свидетельство о словах в Вормсе, ранняя запись эпизода на лестнице или письмо Мюнцера с карьерными мотивами.

components.component: Luther delivered a final answer at Worms refusing to retract, on the grounds that popes and councils have frequently erred and that his conscience is bound by the word of God, closing 'may God help me. Amen.'
components.rationale: The substance of the answer is preserved in the official transcripts (Deutsche Reichstagsakten JR II) and is accepted by the entire field; the GC wording is D'Aubigne's faithful English rendering (local file, lines 40834-40851), and no scholar disputes the content.

components.component: Luther spoke the specific closing sentence 'Here I stand, I can do no other.'
components.rationale: Two contemporaneous witness classes conflict: the notarial on-the-spot transcripts end at 'God help me. Amen' without the sentence, while the earliest Wittenberg printed edition (1521, within weeks) includes it. The majority position for c. 50 years is 'probably not spoken' (Young, Lutheran Quarterly 2022, traces the mythologization; Roper and Schilling treat it as legend-layer), but qualified scholars keep it open on the same evidence: Bainton's standard biography argues the listeners 'may have been too moved to write', and the print originates in circles that heard the speech. Neither side rests on superseded data or circularity; current evidence underdetermines the answer. This is the rare case where 'genuinely-open' is positively documented rather than a default.

rulings.weakened_version: Luther's final Worms answer as quoted is authentic in substance; the closing 'Here I stand, I can do no other' derives from the earliest 1521 printed edition of the speech and is absent from the official transcripts, so it may be an editorial addition rather than his spoken words.
rulings.minority_engagement: Both sides documented per section 1. Against verbatim authenticity: the transcript silence, weighed by most scholars since the mid-20th century as decisive for the verbatim record (state of the question per Young, LQ 2022; the anniversary scholarship of 2021). For authenticity: Bainton (Here I Stand, 1950) and a live minority note the phrase's presence in a contemporaneous print produced by people with access to eyewitnesses, and explain the transcript silence by the tumult at the close of the session. The Phase-1 defender argued exactly this two-contemporaneous-sources shape and is vindicated: the downgrade from anachronistic to disputed was correct, and this adjudication confirms the dispute is genuine rather than settled against the book. Note that D'Aubigne (and hence GC) did not invent the line; its genealogy runs to the 1521 print.

sources.ref: Samuel L. Young, 'How Luther Became the Mythical Here I Stand Hero', Lutheran Quarterly 36 (2022)
sources.independence_note: Peer-reviewed study of the phrase's textual history and reception; independent of Bainton's biographical tradition.

sources.ref: Roland Bainton, Here I Stand: A Life of Martin Luther (1950), on the earliest printed version ('may nevertheless be genuine... too moved to write')
sources.independence_note: Standard academic biography; the named minority position defending possible authenticity; independent reading of the same primary evidence.

sources.ref: Official Worms transcripts (Deutsche Reichstagsakten, Juengere Reihe II) as discussed in 'Martin Luther's Stand at Worms, Uncut' (Concordia Theology, 2021) and CPH 'Did Luther really say Here I stand?'
sources.independence_note: The transcript evidence itself; mediated by confessional-Lutheran academic outlets that nonetheless report the negative finding against their own tradition's icon, which strengthens credibility.

sources.ref: World History Encyclopedia, 'Luther's Speech at the Diet of Worms'
sources.independence_note: Digests Bainton for the authenticity discussion; genealogically dependent on Bainton, counted with him as one line.

sources.ref: D'Aubigne, History of the Reformation, b.7 ch.8 (local file daubigne-history-of-reformation-complete-1849.txt, lines 40834-40851)
sources.independence_note: Genealogy of GC's text: White reproduces D'Aubigne verbatim, who follows the printed-edition tradition.

rulings.would_change_this: An independent contemporaneous eyewitness record (a letter or diary of a Diet attendee, datable to 1521) either containing or explicitly denying the closing sentence would settle the wording question; short of such a find, the dispute stays open.
rulings.notes: Phase-1 defense engaged and adopted; this is the dossier's clearest vindication of the defender.

components.component: Luther devoutly climbed the Scala Sancta ('Pilate's staircase') on his knees in Rome, seeking the promised indulgence.
components.rationale: Attested by Luther himself (sermon of 15 September 1545: he climbed wishing to redeem his grandfather from purgatory and doubted at the top, 'Who knows whether it is true?'); accepted across the scholarship on the Rome journey (Schneider; the De Gruyter volume Martin Luther in Rom).

components.component: During the climb a voice like thunder seemed to say to him 'The just shall live by faith', whereupon he sprang to his feet and fled the place in shame and horror.
components.rationale: The only 16th-century root of the verse-on-the-stairs story is Paul Luther's late, secondhand memorandum (Rudolstadt) of his father's 1544 table narration, and even that account has the verse 'come into his mind', with no thunder-voice, no springing up, no flight in shame and horror; those elements enter the tradition with D'Aubigne's dramatization (local file, lines 12729-12743: 'a voice of thunder crying from the bottom of his heart... He flies far from the scene of his folly'). Luther's own recollections of the staircase record doubt about the indulgence, not a Romans-1:17 revelation, and his 1545 preface dates the justification breakthrough to the Wittenberg years. Modern specialist scholarship (Schneider and the scholars following him: Kaufmann, Moeller, Leppin, Koepf; cf. Brecht vol. 1) doubts any reformatory awakening on the stairs and reads Paul's account as harmonizing. Preponderance is clearly against the scene as narrated, but a well-attested family tradition and Schaff's credence keep a defense alive, so improbable rather than discredited.

rulings.weakened_version: Luther climbed the Scala Sancta seeking the indulgence and later viewed the act with shame; a 16th-century family tradition (Paul Luther's record of his father's 1544 table account) relates that the words 'the just shall live by faith' came to his mind on the stairs.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest opposing position: the Phase-1 defender, backed by Schaff (HCC VII), who reports the Paul Luther attestation and treats it as plausible testimony 'from the lips of his father in 1544'. The defender's three points: (1) the tradition predates D'Aubigne (16th-c. root, Rudolstadt letter) -- conceded, and it is why the weakened kernel is ruled genuinely-open rather than improbable; (2) GC's 'seemed to say to him' is phenomenological -- conceded as a softening, but the claim still narrates an experience-plus-flight event for which no early source exists in that form; (3) GC does not locate the full breakthrough on the stairs -- partially conceded (GC says 'from that time he saw more clearly than ever before'), yet the printed scene (thunder-voice, springing up, flight in shame and horror) is D'Aubigne's theater, absent from Paul Luther's own account, in tension with Luther's attested staircase memory (doubt, grandfather's purgatory), and rejected as a conversion scene by modern specialists. The defense rescues the kernel, not the scene as written.

sources.ref: Philip Schaff, History of the Christian Church VII (via CCEL), on the staircase incident and Paul Luther's 1544 testimony
sources.independence_note: Older standard scholarly reference; carries the pro-kernel position and documents the attestation's provenance; genealogically the same family-tradition line as D'Aubigne but critically aware of it.

sources.ref: Hans Schneider, 'Martin Luthers Reise nach Rom -- neu datiert und neu gedeutet' (Akademie der Wissenschaften zu Goettingen / De Gruyter, 2011); cf. the volume Martin Luther in Rom (De Gruyter, 2017) and its Sehepunkte review 18 (2018) nr. 11
sources.independence_note: Modern specialist scholarship on the Rome journey; independent of the Schaff/D'Aubigne tradition; source of the 1511/12 redating and of the skeptical reading of Paul Luther's harmonizing account.

sources.ref: Luther's sermon of 15 September 1545 ('Who knows whether it is true?', grandfather/purgatory motive) and his 1545 Latin preface (breakthrough dated to the Wittenberg years), as presented in the scholarship above and at luther2017.de ('Luthers Platz im Suendenpfuhl')
sources.independence_note: Luther's own late recollections, read through critical scholarship; genealogically independent of the Paul Luther family line.

sources.ref: Britannica, 'Martin Luther' (tower-experience dating)
sources.independence_note: Digest of the standard biographical scholarship (Brecht line); used only for the breakthrough-dating commonplace.

sources.ref: D'Aubigne, History of the Reformation (local file, lines 12709-12750)
sources.independence_note: Genealogy of GC's scene: the thunder-voice and the flight are D'Aubigne's additions to the family tradition; White compresses him.

rulings.would_change_this: An attestation of the staircase verse-experience earlier than or independent of Paul Luther's memorandum (e.g., a datable letter from Luther's circle before 1544) would move the kernel toward probable; a scholarly demonstration that the Rudolstadt memorandum is inauthentic or misdated would push the whole episode toward discredited.
rulings.notes: Phase-1 defense engaged: its provenance point is incorporated into the weakened ruling; its reading of GC's caution does not save the dramatized scene as printed.

components.component: Luther did not teach or sanction armed rebellion; he opposed the revolt's doctrines and condemned it (implicit substrate of the exculpation).
components.rationale: Documented in Luther's own 1525 writings (Admonition to Peace; Against the Robbing and Murdering Hordes) and unanimous in the scholarship: his two-kingdoms reading of Christian freedom excluded political insurrection, and he denounced the rising.

components.component: The charge that the rebellion was the fruit of Luther's doctrines was without any foundation whatsoever.
components.rationale: The absolute denial fails against documented counterexamples (section 6.3): the Twelve Articles ground the peasants' demands in Scripture and evangelical principle (co-drafted by the evangelical preacher Schappeler with Lotzer); the peasants explicitly invoked Luther's 'freedom of a Christian' and appealed to him by name; and the post-Blickle consensus (The Revolution of 1525; carried into Roper, Summer of Fire and Blood 2025, and OUP's Luther and the Peasants 2025) treats the evangelical message as a constitutive ingredient of the 1525 program. The charge therefore had real foundation in the causal-contribution sense, even though it fails in the intentional/doctrinal-warrant sense. One elastic reading survives (see minority engagement), which caps the ruling at improbable rather than discredited.

rulings.weakened_version: The charge that Luther intended or doctrinally sanctioned the peasants' rebellion was unfounded: he consistently distinguished spiritual from political freedom, urged peace, and condemned the revolt, which the rebels' leaders held against him.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest case for the claim as written: the Phase-1 defender's narrow-reading argument -- 'the charge' (per GC-10-065) is that the rebellion was the LEGITIMATE fruit of Luther's doctrines, i.e. that his teaching doctrinally warranted revolt, and modern scholarship indeed rejects that (the 'tragic misunderstanding' literature: Kolb, 'Luther on Peasants and Princes'; McGath/OLL; the Saeculum article) -- reinforced by the older Guenther Franz school (the revolt rooted in pre-Reformation political-legal traditions, Bundschuh and 'altes Recht') and by Blickle's own observations that peasant-rights ideas predated Luther and that his writings circulated more in towns than villages. Why this fails for the as-written wording: even on the narrow reading, 'without the SLIGHTEST foundation' overshoots, because the peasants' documented appeals to Luther's language gave the princes' charge visible foundation (the defender himself concedes the absolutist adverb is the claim's honest weakness); and the natural 19th-century sense of 'legitimate fruit' is causal ('natural offspring'), a proposition modern historiography substantially affirms rather than rejects. Franz's minimization of the Reformation factor is superseded on this specific point by the post-1975 consensus from Blickle to Roper. The defender's narrow reading is honored in full in the weakened version, which is well-supported.

sources.ref: Peter Blickle, The Revolution of 1525: The German Peasants' War from a New Perspective (Johns Hopkins UP), state of the question via published discussion/review
sources.independence_note: The field-defining monograph on the evangelical framing of the 1525 program ('godly law'); independent of the D'Aubigne/Protestant-apologetic genealogy of GC's claim.

sources.ref: Lyndal Roper, Summer of Fire and Blood: The German Peasants' War (2025), via Providence review; and Luther and the Peasants: Religion, Ritual, and the Revolt of 1525 (Oxford UP, 2025)
sources.independence_note: Current specialist scholarship, genealogically independent of Blickle's school; converges on the Reformation-entanglement of the revolt.

sources.ref: Twelve Articles of Memmingen (1525) and Luther's Admonition to Peace / Against the Robbing and Murdering Hordes, read through the above scholarship and standard encyclopedic digests (Wikipedia 'Twelve Articles', 'German Peasants' War'; World History Encyclopedia)
sources.independence_note: Primary texts establishing both the evangelical grounding of the peasant program (against the absolute) and Luther's condemnation of the revolt (for the weakened version).

sources.ref: Robert Kolb, 'Luther on Peasants and Princes' (Lutheran Quarterly); McGath, 'The Peasants' War and Martin Luther' (OLL); Saeculum journal article on Luther and the gospel of social unrest
sources.independence_note: The exculpatory 'tragic misunderstanding' line, partly confessional-Lutheran in location; weighed as the named counter-position, and it carries the weakened version.

sources.ref: Guenther Franz, Der deutsche Bauernkrieg (older standard), via historiographical surveys
sources.independence_note: Older school minimizing the Reformation factor; superseded on this point by Blickle and successors but documents that qualified scholarship once leaned closer to GC's framing.

rulings.would_change_this: A peer-reviewed synthesis demonstrating that the 1525 program formed independently of evangelical preaching (e.g., establishing that the Twelve Articles' scriptural framing was a late instrumental veneer over purely agrarian-legal demands) would rehabilitate the as-written denial; conversely, evidence that Luther privately encouraged resistance would destroy even the weakened version, and nothing of the kind exists.
rulings.notes: Phase-1 defense engaged: its narrow reading is adopted as the weakened version and ruled well-supported; its own concession on 'not the slightest' is decisive for the as-written ruling. Period-standard genealogy confirmed: D'Aubigne's exculpation (local file, lines 62073-62078) is the claim's source layer.

components.component: Muntzer was driven by ambition to obtain position and influence (motive attribution).
components.rationale: No document from Muntzer's hand evidences office- or status-seeking as his motive; the contemporary attributions of pride and ambition come from his adversaries (Luther's polemics; Melanchthon's Histori Thome Muntzers, 1525), which scholarship treats as hostile genre pieces rather than history (Friesen, Church History: Melanchthon composed it as a humanist oratorical exemplar showing revolution as the natural fruit of Schwaermerei). The rival motive account -- apocalyptic-mystical theology, election of the Spirit-taught, imminent judgment -- has direct documentary support in Muntzer's own corpus (Prague Manifesto 1521, Sermon to the Princes 1524, Vindication and Refutation 1524, published letters; Matheson's Collected Works 1988; Franz's critical edition 1968) and carries the specialist consensus (Scott 1989; Goertz 1989/2015; Braeuer-Vogler 2016; Drummond 2024). Under the amended section 3 symmetry rule the undocumented motive attribution caps at improbable because the rival account is documentary; on the merits the preponderance independently supports the same ruling.

components.component: Muntzer was unwilling to be second, even to Luther (characterization of his stance toward Wittenberg).
components.rationale: The behavioral core is documented in Muntzer's own writings: he refused subordination to Wittenberg, claimed a divine commission superseding Luther's reform, and attacked Luther personally ('Doctor Liar', 'the soft-living flesh of Wittenberg'). As a status idiom ('unwilling to be second') the phrase glosses this documented refusal in rivalry terms that Muntzer himself framed theologically, so it is probable as a characterization rather than well-supported.

rulings.weakened_version: Muntzer assertively refused any position subordinate to Luther, claiming a divinely commissioned reform superior to Wittenberg's, and contemporaries beginning with Luther and Melanchthon read this self-assertion as ambition.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest opposing (pro-claim) positions. (a) The Phase-1 defender's three arguments: (1) the sentence sits in a paragraph that immediately supplies Muntzer's theology (GC-10-055/056), so White lists ambition alongside doctrine, not instead of it -- answered: adjudication rules on the sentence as printed, and the printed sentence asserts a determinate psychological motive that the documentary record does not support; accurate theology nearby does not document the ambition motive. (2) Internal consistency with GC-10-052/053, which Phase 1 marked 'supported' as subjective temperament judgments -- answered: Phase-1 verdicts bind nothing here (charter section 0.2), and 054 differs in kind: 'ambitious to obtain position and influence' is a checkable motive attribution contradicted by specialist biography, not a vague temperament remark. (3) The behavioral core (refusal to be second) is confirmed by modern scholarship -- conceded in full; that is exactly the weakened version, ruled well-supported. (b) The older historiographical line descending from Melanchthon's Histori through 19th-century Protestant historiography (D'Aubigne: 'dissatisfied with the inferior part he was playing'), for which the rivalry-as-driver reading was standard -- answered: the fact of the rupture is undeniable, but the ambition reading fails on genre and bias per section 4.2 (its documentary base is adversary polemic, so read by Friesen and the specialists), and it is superseded by work on Muntzer's own corpus. Confessional location of any party plays no role in these dismissals; the polemics fail as motive evidence because of genre, not because Luther and Melanchthon were parties to the conflict per se.

sources.ref: Tom Scott, Thomas Muntzer: Theology and Revolution in the German Reformation (1989)
sources.independence_note: Anglophone specialist biography grounding Muntzer's course in theology and social context; independent of the German radical-Reformation school.

sources.ref: Hans-Juergen Goertz, Thomas Muntzer: Apocalyptic Mystic and Revolutionary (Eng. 1993; rev. German 2015)
sources.independence_note: German specialist tradition, genealogically independent of Scott; converges on apocalyptic-mystical motivation.

sources.ref: Abraham Friesen, 'Philipp Melanchthon, Wilhelm Zimmermann and the Dilemma of Muntzer Historiography', Church History (Cambridge)
sources.independence_note: Peer-reviewed historiographical study establishing the genre and polemical purpose of Melanchthon's Histori, the fountainhead of the ambition portrait GC inherits via D'Aubigne.

sources.ref: Muntzer's own writings and letters (Prague Manifesto 1521; Sermon to the Princes 1524; Vindication and Refutation 1524; Matheson, Collected Works 1988; Franz critical edition 1968)
sources.independence_note: The documentary base of the rival motive account, read through the specialist editions; also documents the behavioral core of the weakened version (refusal of subordination, attacks on Luther).

sources.ref: Andrew Drummond, The Dreadful History and Judgement of God on Thomas Muntzer (2024), via The Nation and Cleveland Review of Books reviews; Duke UP History of the Present 15/1 (2025) special-issue introduction
sources.independence_note: Current reassessment literature; used as convergent positions on the reception history, not as arbiters.

sources.ref: D'Aubigne, History of the Reformation (local file, lines 62215-62233: 'dissatisfied with the inferior part he was playing')
sources.independence_note: Genealogy of GC's sentence: White's 'unwilling to be second, even to Luther' renders D'Aubigne's phrase; the ambition frame is period-standard, matching the Phase-1 anachronistic diagnosis.

rulings.would_change_this: Publication of a document from Muntzer's hand or immediate circle evidencing office- or status-seeking as his operative motive (e.g., correspondence negotiating rank or precedence vis-a-vis Wittenberg) would rehabilitate the as-written motive; a specialist re-evaluation of Melanchthon's Histori as reliable testimony would also move it toward genuinely-open.
rulings.notes: Motive-cap arithmetic per amended section 3: rival account documented in Muntzer's own corpus, so the improbable cap applies; the ruling also holds on preponderance alone.
